sections.summary: Title only: Standard for Onions and Shallots (no substantive provisions in this heading).
Document identifier and adoption year (CXS 348-2022, adopted 2022); not a substantive regulatory provision.

Defines the scope: sets quality requirements for onions and shallots after preparation and packaging, allows slight lack of freshness/turgidity or slight deterioration post‑packaging, and obliges the holder/seller to market only produce conforming with the standard.

Defines covered produce as onion bulbs (Allium cepa L. Cepa group), shallot bulbs (Allium cepa Aggregatum group) and grey shallots (Allium oschaninii), excludes green/onions for industrial processing, and specifies permitted shapes (round/oval/long/demi‑long) and skin colour characteristics.

Sets provisions concerning quality including minimum requirements (wholeness, firmness, soundness, cleanliness, pest freedom, dryness, absence of abnormal moisture/odours, pseudostem length limits), minimum maturity and transport suitability, and classification into three classes (Extra, I, II) with class‑specific allowed defects.

Lists mandatory minimum quality criteria applicable to all classes: whole, firm, sound (no rotting), clean, practically free from pests and pest damage, no hollow/tough pseudostems, no visible shoot growth, no abnormal external moisture, no off‑odours/tastes, no temperature damage, sufficiently dry for intended use with pseudostem twist/cut ≤6 cm (exceptions for strings); pest provisions subject to IPPC rules.

Requires onions and shallots to be sufficiently developed for their variety/commercial type and growing area, firm with dry papery outer skins, and in condition to withstand transport and arrive in satisfactory condition at destination.
Establishes three quality classes—Extra, Class I and Class II—with specific allowable defects and criteria detailed in the following subsections.

Extra class: produce of superior quality, characteristic of variety/commercial type, essentially free from defects except very slight superficial ones that do not affect appearance/quality/keeping; shallot multiple bulbs are not a defect, very slight shape/colour defects and very light staining (≤20% surface) allowed, but onions must be free from splits/double centres; root tufts permitted for grey shallots and for immaturely harvested bulbs.

Class I: good quality produce characteristic of the variety with limited allowable defects (slight shape/colour defects, superficial cracks or partial absence of outer skins if flesh protected, light staining ≤33% surface, double/triple bulbs for onions allowed, slight glassiness limited to outer fleshy ring, root tufts allowed for grey/immature harvests) and practically free of sprouting.

Class II: includes produce meeting only the minimum requirements, allowing greater defects (shape/colour defects, slight bruising, healed mechanical damage, root tufts, stains ≤50% surface, cracks/partial skin absence ≤33% surface if flesh undamaged), slight glassiness up to two outer rings and double/triple bulbs, and practically free of sprouting.

Provides sizing provisions: onions minimum diameter 10 mm with within‑package size variation limits (10/20/30 mm depending on size bands) and requirement to label package with size and sizing method; shallots minimum diameter 10 mm, maximum diameters 55 mm (round) or 60 mm (demi‑long/long), and specific within‑package variation limits by size ranges.

Specifies tolerances: lots may include a percentage of produce not satisfying the indicated class but meeting lower classes (Extra: 5% allowed nonconforming but meeting Class I with 1% decay limit; Class I: 10% allowed nonconforming but meeting Class II with 1% decay and up to 4% firm bulbs with ≤1 cm shoot growth; Class II: 10% allowed failing minimum with 2% decay and up to 10% firm bulbs with ≤1 cm shoot growth), allows re‑sorting to achieve conformity per CXG 47-2003, and a 10% size tolerance for off‑size bulbs.

Details quality tolerances at all marketing stages: Extra class allows 5% not meeting the class but meeting Class I (including 1% decay), Class I allows 10% not meeting the class but meeting Class II (including 1% decay) and up to 4% with external shoot growth ≤1 cm, and Class II allows 10% not meeting the class or minimum requirements (including 2% decay) and up to 10% with shoot growth ≤1 cm; failed lots may be re‑sorted per CXG 47-2003.
Allows up to 5% by number or weight of onions and shallots that do not meet Extra class but meet Class I, with up to 1% allowed for decay, soft rot and/or internal breakdown.

Allows up to 10% by number or weight of onions and shallots that do not meet Class I but meet Class II, including up to 1% for decay/soft rot/internal breakdown, and additionally permits up to 4% of firm bulbs to show externally visible shoot growth not exceeding 1 cm.

Allows up to 10% by number or weight of onions and shallots that meet neither Class II nor the minimum requirements, including up to 2% for decay/soft rot/internal breakdown, and additionally permits up to 10% of firm bulbs to show externally visible shoot growth not exceeding 1.0 cm.
For all classes, if produce is sized, allows up to 10% by number or weight of onions and shallots not corresponding to the size indicated on the package.

Sets presentation provisions: packages/lots must be uniform by origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size with the visible portion representative of the whole, mixtures of distinct commercial types/colours are permitted in consumer packages if uniform in quality and origin (size uniformity not required); requires packing to protect produce using food‑grade materials, allows braided tops, and mandates containers meet quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance criteria and be free of foreign matter and smell, with compliance to CXC 44‑1995.

Requires each package or bulk lot to be uniform in origin, variety/commercial type, quality and size, permits mixtures of distinct commercial types/colours in consumer packages if uniform in quality and origin (size uniformity not required), and mandates the visible part be representative of the entire contents.

Requires onions and shallots to be packed to protect the produce using clean, food‑grade materials that do not cause damage, permits tops braided or interlaced on strings, and requires packing to comply with CXC 44‑1995.

Containers must satisfy quality, hygiene, ventilation and resistance characteristics to ensure proper handling, shipping and preservation of onions and shallots, and packages must be free of foreign matter and odours.
Section heading only; substantive marking and labelling provisions appear in the following subsections.
Adds specific labelling provisions for consumer packages that supplement the General Standard for the Labelling of Pre‑packaged Foods (CXS 1‑1985).
If the produce is not visible from outside, each consumer package must be labelled with the name of the produce ('onions' or 'shallots' as defined) and may indicate variety and/or commercial type.

Requires declaration of country of origin and optionally district or place name, and for mixtures of distinctly different varieties or commercial types from different origins the country of origin must be indicated next to each relevant variety/commercial type/colour.
Labelling of non‑retail containers should follow CXS 346‑2021 and each package must bear specified particulars legibly and indelibly on the same side, visible from the outside.

Requires country of origin (optionally district/place) on non‑retail containers and mandates that for mixtures of different commercial types/colours from different origins each country of origin be shown next to the corresponding type/colour.
Non‑retail containers must indicate class, may indicate variety and/or commercial type, and must show size if sized (expressed as minimum and maximum diameters or by the method used).
Provides for an optional official inspection mark.
Prohibits the use of any food additives in onions and shallots.
Requires the produce to comply with Codex maximum residue limits for pesticides and with maximum levels set in the General Standard for Contaminants and Toxins in Food and Feed (CXS 193‑1995).

Recommends preparing and handling the produce in accordance with Codex hygiene texts (General Principles of Food Hygiene CXC 1‑1969, Code of Hygienic Practice for Fresh Fruits and Vegetables CXC 53‑2003, etc.) and states the produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21‑1997.

sections.section: 10.2 The produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with the Principles and Guidelines for the Establishment and Application of Microbiological Criteria Related to Foods (CXG 21-1997).
sections.summary: States that the produce should comply with any microbiological criteria established in accordance with CXG 21‑1997.
